items.title: Post-Training in End-to-End Autonomous Driving
items.topic: Post-Training in End-to-End Autonomous Driving
items.summary: Researchers on arXiv have proposed a post-training approach for end-to-end autonomous driving models, which aims to address the limitations of traditional machine learning applications in safety-critical and interaction-intensive environments, such as autonomous vehicles, by incorporating enhancements that enable the model to handle occlusions and recover from execution errors, and is relevant for AV research as it has the potential to improve the reliability and safety of autonomous vehicles. The approach is designed to handle the challenges of autonomous driving, including the accumulation of small execution errors and the scarcity of recovery behaviors in training data. With its focus on post-training, this approach has the potential to enhance the performance of autonomous vehicles in real-world scenarios. The proposed approach is evaluated on its ability to handle occlusions and recover from execution errors.

items.title: Automating Quality Assessment with NLP of LLM-Generated Defeaters
items.topic: Automating Quality Assessment with NLP of LLM-Generated Defeaters
items.summary: Researchers from arXiv have developed an automated approach for assessing the quality of LLM-generated defeaters, which are potential challenges to safety claims in high-integrity systems such as autonomous vehicle fleets, using natural language processing techniques that combine structural features from assurance case graphs with semantic embeddings and meta-classifiers, as evaluated through expert-assessed defeater annotations, which is critical for ensuring the reliability and trustworthiness of autonomous systems, and has direct relevance to AV research by enhancing the validation of safety claims.

items.title: VLM-CASE: Vision-Language Model Enabled Context-Adaptive Safety Envelopes for Anticipatory Safe Autonomous Driving
items.topic: VLM-CASE: Vision-Language Model Enabled Context-Adaptive Safety Envelopes for Anticipatory Safe Autonomous Driving
items.summary: VLM-CASE, a framework proposed by arXiv researchers, enables anticipatory safe autonomous driving by integrating a vision-language model with a formal safety model, allowing the vehicle to reason about the scene and adjust its motion accordingly, with the model fine-tuned using low-rank adaptation, and tested on front-camera images to report road surface and visibility conditions, which is crucial for handling adverse driving conditions such as bad weather, and has the potential to significantly improve autonomous vehicle safety and reliability.

items.title: CritiqueDriveVLM: From Verifier-Guided Reinforcement Learning to Latent Thought Distillation for Autonomous Driving
items.topic: CritiqueDriveVLM: From Verifier-Guided Reinforcement Learning to Latent Thought Distillation for Autonomous Driving
items.summary: CritiqueDriveVLM, a novel framework proposed by arXiv researchers on July 5, 2026, internalizes reasoning directly into a Vision-Language Model (VLM) for autonomous driving, using Critique-Driven Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning (RL) guided by a multi-dimensional verifier, which provides granular scalar feedback and a multi-turn penalty, forcing the policy to internalize reasoning and mitigate issues such as reasoning hallucinations and conservative biases, and has the potential to significantly improve the reliability and efficiency of autonomous vehicles.

items.title: LLM-Empowered Multimodal Fusion Framework for Autonomous Driving: Semantic Enhancement and Channel-Adaptive Design
items.topic: LLM-Empowered Multimodal Fusion Framework for Autonomous Driving: Semantic Enhancement and Channel-Adaptive Design
items.summary: The arXiv paper LLM-Empowered Multimodal Fusion Framework for Autonomous Driving proposes a Large Language Model-centric Semantic-layer Channel-aware Integrated Perception (LM-SCIP) framework, which fuses a local visual stream with a quality-varying external radar stream to cover perception-blind spots. The framework couples a hierarchical radar-vision encoder with a Channel-Adaptive decoder to achieve robust autonomous driving. This approach is relevant to AV research as it enhances the fusion of multimodal data, such as vision and radar, to improve the perception and decision-making capabilities of autonomous vehicles. The use of a Large Language Model as a central reasoning core is a key innovation in this framework.

items.title: What's Hidden Matters: Identifying Planning-Critical Occluded Agents using Vision-Language Models
items.topic: What's Hidden Matters: Identifying Planning-Critical Occluded Agents using Vision-Language Models
items.summary: The arXiv paper 'What's Hidden Matters: Identifying Planning-Critical Occluded Agents using Vision-Language Models' proposes a novel framework for identifying and reasoning about occluded agents that are critical to the ego-vehicle's trajectory. The framework uses Planning KL-divergence (PKL) to systematically identify and rank occluded agents based on their impact on the ego vehicle's plan. This framework is relevant for autonomous driving as it enables better understanding of hidden hazards and risks. The paper was published on 2026-07-01.

items.title: TrafficAlign: Aligning Large Language Models for Traffic Scenario Generation
items.topic: TrafficAlign: Aligning Large Language Models for Traffic Scenario Generation
items.summary: The arXiv-published paper TrafficAlign, dated June 27, 2026, presents an automated framework that synthesizes traffic scenarios based on real-world driving videos and aligns large language models with these scenarios. TrafficAlign generates traffic scenarios that are highly effective in revealing collisions, with up to 10.8% more collisions on average across three autonomous driving models compared to state-of-the-art methods. The fine-tuning of driving models with TrafficAlign-generated scenarios reduces collision rates by 36.1%. This work is relevant to autonomous driving as it enhances the safety and performance of autonomous vehicles by improving their ability to handle various traffic scenarios.
items.abstract: The arXiv-published paper TrafficAlign, dated June 27, 2026, presents an automated framework that synthesizes traffic scenarios based on real-world driving videos and aligns large language models with these scenarios. TrafficAlign generates traffic scenarios that are highly effective in revealing collisions, with up to 10.8% more collisions on average across three autonomous driving models compared to state-of-the-art methods. The fine-tuning of driving models with TrafficAlign-generated scenarios reduces collision rates by 36.1%. This work is relevant to autonomous driving as it enhances the safety and performance of autonomous vehicles by improving their ability to handle various traffic scenarios.

items.title: LAMP: Lane-Aligned Motion Primitives for Feasible Trajectory Prediction
items.topic: LAMP: Lane-Aligned Motion Primitives for Feasible Trajectory Prediction
items.summary: LAMP, a framework proposed by arXiv researchers, is a topology-aware forecasting framework that anchors multimodal prediction to structured motion primitives aligned with lane topology. LAMP uses a VQ-VAE to learn shape-aware motion primitives as discrete intention queries, capturing the adherence to lane topology of multimodal predictions. The framework's design enables it to predict trajectories that are physically and logically consistent, making it a significant advancement in motion forecasting for autonomous driving. LAMP's approach is particularly critical in safety-sensitive domains such as autonomous driving, where accurate trajectory prediction is essential for safe decision-making and planning.

The framework's design enables it to predict trajectories that are physically and logically consistent, making it a significant advancement in motion forecasting for autonomous driving. LAMP's approach is particularly critical in safety-sensitive domains such as autonomous driving, where accurate trajectory prediction is essential for safe decision-making and planning.

items.title: Intend, Reflect, Refine: An Adaptive Multimodal Reflection Framework for Autonomous Driving
items.topic: Intend, Reflect, Refine: An Adaptive Multimodal Reflection Framework for Autonomous Driving
items.summary: The arXiv paper 'Intend, Reflect, Refine: An Adaptive Multimodal Reflection Framework for Autonomous Driving' presents a novel framework for autonomous driving that combines high-level reasoning with physical constraints. The authors propose IRR-Drive, a framework that generates a preliminary textual intention and anticipates potential interactions by predicting future semantic bird's-eye view (BEV) representations. This approach enables the framework to reflect on its decisions and refine its trajectory planning. The authors evaluate IRR-Drive on its ability to generate safe and efficient driving trajectories in complex scenarios. The results demonstrate that IRR-Drive achieves competitive performance with state-of-the-art models while providing improved interpretability and explainability.

items.title: OmniSpace: Efficient Geometry Awareness for Autonomous Vehicles MLLMs
items.topic: OmniSpace: Efficient Geometry Awareness for Autonomous Vehicles MLLMs
items.summary: The arXiv paper 'OmniSpace: Efficient Geometry Awareness for Autonomous Vehicles MLLMs' introduces a new paradigm for geometry-aware spatial reasoning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs). The authors propose OmniSpace, a plug-and-play module that enhances the spatial intelligence of MLLMs using a Camera Pose Injector, a Multi-view Epipolar Attention module, and a 3D Geometric Distillation objective. This approach enables MLLMs to reason about 3D geometry from 2D observations, addressing a key challenge in autonomous driving applications. The authors evaluate OmniSpace on its ability to improve the performance of MLLMs in various tasks, including 3D object detection and scene understanding. The results demonstrate that OmniSpace achieves significant improvements in geometry-aware reasoning.

ParkingTransformer is based on the ability of LLMs to understand and generate human-like language, which can be used to represent and reason about complex scenes.

items.topic: RT-VLA: Real-Time Vision-Language-Action Models via Knowledge Distillation
items.summary: The arXiv-published paper RT-VLA, dated June 12, 2026, introduces a real-time vision-language-action model that enables end-to-end autonomous driving through knowledge distillation, reducing inference latency while preserving language-based reasoning and supporting post-hoc explanation. This model, built upon the SimLingo model, achieves compactness through multi-level supervised distillation. The RT-VLA model is significant for autonomous driving as it addresses the need for real-time control without compromising explainability. By transferring driving and reasoning capabilities into a lightweight model, RT-VLA contributes to the development of more efficient and trustworthy autonomous vehicles.

items.topic: LUNA-AD: Lightweight Uncertainty-Aware Language Model with Lifelong Learning for Autonomous Driving
items.summary: LUNA-AD, a lightweight uncertainty-aware language model with lifelong learning for autonomous driving, was proposed by researchers to address the challenges of integrating large language models into safety-critical driving systems. LUNA-AD features a tri-system architecture that reconciles complex multimodal behavioral reasoning, efficient deployment, and continual refinement. The model is designed to generate uncertainty-aware decision-making demonstrations through diverse hypothesis exploration. LUNA-AD's dual-head lightweight heuristic model unifies the inference of decision distributions and textual explanations, published on arXiv on 2026-06-07, with a focus on improving the reasoning capabilities of autonomous vehicles.

items.title: Output Type Before Quality: A Standards-Derived XAI Admissibility Rubric for Autonomous-Driving Safety
items.topic: Output Type Before Quality: A Standards-Derived XAI Admissibility Rubric for Autonomous-Driving Safety
items.summary: The arXiv paper titled Output Type Before Quality, published on June 3, 2026, derives an XAI admissibility rubric for autonomous-driving safety from standards such as AMLAS, ISO 26262, ISO21448, and ISO/PAS 8800. This work identifies a mismatch between the output types of XAI methods and the evidence required by safety standards, known as the evidence-type gap. The paper evaluates six XAI method classes against 19 testable evidentiary criteria, finding that causal XAI emerges as a promising approach. This research is crucial for ensuring the safety and explainability of autonomous driving systems, as it provides a framework for assessing the admissibility of XAI methods in the context of safety standards.

items.title: Lost in Fog: Sensor Perturbations Expose Reasoning Fragility in Driving VLAs
items.topic: Lost in Fog: Sensor Perturbations Expose Reasoning Fragility in Driving VLAs
items.summary: The arXiv paper 'Lost in Fog: Sensor Perturbations Expose Reasoning Fragility in Driving VLAs' presents a controlled perturbation study of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) robustness in autonomous driving. The authors evaluate Alpamayo R1 across 1,996 scenarios under eight sensor perturbations and find that reasoning consistency is a high-fidelity indicator of trajectory reliability. The study reveals that when Chain-of-Causation (CoC) explanations change after perturbation, trajectory deviation spikes 5.3 times. This work is relevant for AV research as it highlights the importance of robustness and reliability in autonomous driving systems. The paper was published on May 20, 2026.

items.title: From Prompts to Pavement Through Time: Temporal Grounding in Agentic Scene-to-Plan Reasoning
items.topic: From Prompts to Pavement Through Time: Temporal Grounding in Agentic Scene-to-Plan Reasoning
items.summary: The arXiv paper 'From Prompts to Pavement Through Time: Temporal Grounding in Agentic Scene-to-Plan Reasoning' explores the use of temporal conditioning within inter-agent communication to preserve coherence in reasoning about continuous actions in autonomous vehicles. The authors introduce three planner architectures with progressively increasing temporal integration and evaluate them on curated subsets of the BDD-X dataset using semantic, syntactic, and logical metrics. The results show that temporal grounding can enhance coherence without introducing degradation in semantic or logical consistency. The paper was published on May 19, 2026. This work is relevant for improving the safety and interpretability of autonomous driving systems.

items.topic: Towards Trustworthy and Explainable AI for Perception Models: From Concept to Prototype Vehicle Deployment
items.summary: The arXiv paper 'Towards Trustworthy and Explainable AI for Perception Models: From Concept to Prototype Vehicle Deployment' presents a trustworthy AI perception module that integrates faithful explainability and calibrated uncertainty estimates, building on a transformer-based detector. This module derives explanations from the attention mechanism at inference time and validates their faithfulness using perturbation-based consistency tests. The proposed approach addresses the gap in concrete implementations of trustworthy AI for 3D scene understanding. The work is relevant for autonomous driving safety assurance, debugging, and human oversight. The paper was published on May 15, 2026.

items.topic: Handling the Hidden: Occlusion-Aware Motion Prediction for Autonomous Vehicles
items.source: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
items.summary: Fengze Yang, Bo Yu, Yang Zhou, Xuewen Luo, Zhengzhong Tu, and Chenxi Liu have proposed an edge-based multimodal sensor data fusion approach using a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model for real-time autonomous vehicle accident avoidance, as published in the Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence journal. Their approach enables safer navigation by systematically detecting, processing, and anticipating the behavior of occluded areas. The system is evaluated using the CARLA simulator, demonstrating its effectiveness in handling occlusions and improving situational awareness. The proposed approach has the potential to enhance the safety and efficiency of autonomous vehicles in complex environments.

items.title: Socially Compatible and Risk-Aware Driving: A Cognition-Inspired Interactive Decision-Making Framework for Autonomous Vehicles
items.topic: Socially Compatible and Risk-Aware Driving: A Cognition-Inspired Interactive Decision-Making Framework for Autonomous Vehicles
items.source: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
items.summary: The IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems published a paper on Socially Compatible and Risk-Aware Driving: A Cognition-Inspired Interactive Decision-Making Framework for Autonomous Vehicles on March 24, 2026, which proposes a cognitively and socially grounded interactive decision-making framework for autonomous vehicles to address the challenges of risk-aware and socially compatible behaviors in complex mixed traffic environments. The architecture integrates a Traffic Situation Risk Field (TSRF) module, which assesses environmental risks from a social-cognitive perspective. This framework enables autonomous vehicles to exhibit risk-aware and socially compatible behaviors, ensuring safety and efficiency in interactions with human-driven vehicles.

items.title: LLM and AI Agents for Autonomous Systems: A Survey of Applications, Datasets, and Security Challenges
items.topic: LLM and AI Agents for Autonomous Systems: A Survey of Applications, Datasets, and Security Challenges
items.source: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
items.summary: The IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems has published a survey on the applications, datasets, and security challenges of Large Language Models (LLMs) and AI agents in autonomous systems, with a focus on autonomous driving and unmanned aerial vehicles. The survey reviews the role of LLMs in adaptive decision-making, context-aware planning, and human-aligned interaction. Researchers from the journal highlight the importance of LLMs in safety-sensitive domains, such as autonomous driving. The survey provides a comprehensive review of LLM reasoning and AI agents across various applications, including scenario generation, decision-making, and cooperative V2X interactions.

items.summary: The 2026 IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems published a paper by Fengze Yang and colleagues, introducing an edge-based multimodal sensor data fusion approach with a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model for real-time autonomous vehicle accident avoidance, achieving improved performance in complex driving scenarios. This approach combines vision, language, and action modalities to enhance situational awareness and decision-making. The VLA model is designed to handle real-time data from various sensors and cameras, enabling more accurate and efficient accident avoidance. The proposed system has been evaluated on a range of driving scenarios, demonstrating its potential for improving autonomous vehicle safety.
